chunk_summary: This section discusses age-structured differential diagnosis of chronic diarrhea in India, highlighting nutrition-critical red flags and tailored interventions for infants, toddlers, and adolescents, with a focus on early referral and diagnostic integrity.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific differential diagnoses for chronic diarrhea in infants, toddlers, and adolescents.
Identify nutrition-critical red flags requiring urgent intervention or referral.
Apply safe nutrition strategies while awaiting definitive diagnostic results.
Recognize systemic and iatrogenic contributors to chronic diarrhea across all age groups.
key_facts: In early infancy, profuse watery diarrhea with failure to thrive may indicate congenital diarrheal disorders or severe allergy phenotypes.
Toddlers with functional diarrhea often benefit from reducing sweetened beverages and increasing dietary fat and fiber.
School-age children with growth failure, delayed puberty, and anemia should be evaluated for celiac disease and IBD, keeping gluten in the diet until testing is complete.
Chronic infections, including opportunistic pathogens in HIV, should be considered across all ages.
Early referral to tertiary pediatric gastroenterology is critical for infants with severe symptoms to prevent progression to intestinal failure.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Early infancy: profuse watery diarrhea beginning in the neonatal period, persistence despite fasting, recurrent severe dehydration, or early failure to thrive suggests congenital diarrheal disorders, immunodeficiency-related enteropathy, or severe allergy phenotypes. (Source: https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-030-STG-persistent-diarrhea.pdf)
document_key_topics: Age-specific differential diagnosis of chronic diarrhea
Nutrition-critical red flags in pediatric GI disorders
Safe nutrition strategies during diagnostic evaluation
Systemic and iatrogenic contributors to diarrhea
